data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-11T14:54:24.941Z</current_datetime>

We just had a prod incident traced back to tests that were passing but not actually verifying anything.
Can you audit this test class and flag anything suspicious?

```csharp
using Microsoft.VisualStudio.TestTools.UnitTesting;

namespace UserRepo.Tests;

[TestClass]
public class UserRepositoryTests
{
    private static readonly HttpClient _client = new();

    [TestMethod]
    public void GetUser_ExistingId_ReturnsUser()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        repo.Add(new User(1, "Alice"));
        var user = repo.GetById(1);
        Assert.IsNotNull(user);
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void GetUser_NonExistentId_ReturnsNull()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        var user = repo.GetById(999);
        // should be null
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void AddUser_DuplicateId_ThrowsException()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        repo.Add(new User(1, "Alice"));
        try
        {
            repo.Add(new User(1, "Bob"));
            // If we get here, no exception was thrown
        }
        catch (Exception)
        {
            return; // test passes
        }
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void DeleteUser_RemovesFromRepository()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        repo.Add(new User(1, "Alice"));
        repo.Delete(1);
        Assert.AreEqual(repo.Count, repo.Count);
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void UpdateUser_ChangesName()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        repo.Add(new User(1, "Alice"));
        repo.Update(1, "Alicia");
        var user = repo.GetById(1);
        Assert.IsTrue(user.Name != "Alice");
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void GetAllUsers_ReturnsCorrectCount()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        repo.Add(new User(1, "Alice"));
        repo.Add(new User(2, "Bob"));
        repo.Add(new User(3, "Charlie"));
        var all = repo.GetAll();
        Assert.AreEqual(3, all.Count);
        Assert.IsTrue(all.Any(u => u.Name == "Alice"));
        Assert.IsTrue(all.Any(u => u.Name == "Bob"));
        Assert.IsTrue(all.Any(u => u.Name == "Charlie"));
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void AddUser_NullUser_ThrowsArgumentNullException()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        Assert.ThrowsException<Exception>(() => repo.Add(null!));
    }

    [TestMethod]
    public void SearchUsers_ByPartialName_FindsMatches()
    {
        var repo = new UserRepository();
        repo.Add(new User(1, "Alice"));
        repo.Add(new User(2, "Alicia"));
        repo.Add(new User(3, "Bob"));
        var results = repo.Search("Ali");
        Assert.IsTrue(results.Count == 2);
        Console.WriteLine($"Found {results.Count} users matching 'Ali'");
    }
}
```

```csharp
namespace UserRepo;

public record User(int Id, string Name);

public class UserRepository
{
    private readonly Dictionary<int, User> _users = new();

    public void Add(User user)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(user);
        if (_users.ContainsKey(user.Id))
            throw new InvalidOperationException($"User {user.Id} already exists");
        _users[user.Id] = user;
    }

    public User? GetById(int id) => _users.GetValueOrDefault(id);
    public void Delete(int id) => _users.Remove(id);
    public void Update(int id, string name) => _users[id] = _users[id] with { Name = name };
    public List<User> GetAll() => _users.Values.ToList();
    public List<User> Search(string partial) => _users.Values.Where(u => u.Name.Contains(partial)).ToList();
    public int Count => _users.Count;
}
```

data.content:
Here's the audit. I found **6 problematic tests** out of 8 — several of which would directly explain your prod incident:

### 🔴 Critical — Tests that never fail

| Test | Problem |
|---|---|
| `GetUser_NonExistentId_ReturnsNull` | **No assertion at all.** The comment `// should be null` isn't code. Always passes. |
| `AddUser_DuplicateId_ThrowsException` | **Missing `Assert.Fail()` on the happy path.** If no exception is thrown, the test silently passes. Use `Assert.ThrowsException<InvalidOperationException>()` instead. |
| `DeleteUser_RemovesFromRepository` | **Tautological assertion.** `Assert.AreEqual(repo.Count, repo.Count)` compares the value to itself. Always passes. Should be `Assert.AreEqual(0, repo.Count)`. |

### 🟡 Weak — Tests that pass but verify the wrong thing

| Test | Problem |
|---|---|
| `GetUser_ExistingId_ReturnsUser` | **Shallow assertion.** `IsNotNull` doesn't verify it returned the *right* user. Should assert `user.Id == 1` and `user.Name == "Alice"`. |
| `UpdateUser_ChangesName` | **Negation-only check.** `user.Name != "Alice"` would pass if the name were `""` or any garbage. Assert the *expected* value: `AreEqual("Alicia", user.Name)`. |
| `AddUser_NullUser_ThrowsArgumentNullException` | **Wrong exception type.** Catches base `Exception` but method name promises `ArgumentNullException`. Use `ThrowsException<ArgumentNullException>()`. |

### 🟣 Minor — Style/smell

| Issue | Location |
|---|---|
| **Unused `HttpClient`** field — dead code or sign of leaked real HTTP calls. | Line 8 |
| `SearchUsers` uses `Assert.IsTrue(x == 2)` instead of `Assert.AreEqual(2, x)` — failure message will be useless ("IsTrue failed"). | `SearchUsers_ByPartialName_FindsMatches` |

### ✅ Clean
`GetAllUsers_ReturnsCorrectCount` — good count check plus content verification.

---

The three critical tests are almost certainly your culprit — they literally cannot fail regardless of what the production code does.
data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to audit their test class for issues. Let me analyze each test method carefully against the production code.

Let me go through each test:

1. **GetUser_ExistingId_ReturnsUser** - Only asserts `IsNotNull`. Doesn't verify it's the *right* user (id=1, name="Alice"). Shallow assertion.

2. **GetUser_NonExistentId_ReturnsNull** - Has NO assertion at all. The comment `// should be null` is not an assertion. This test always passes.

3. **AddUser_DuplicateId_ThrowsException** - Uses try/catch instead of `Assert.ThrowsException`.
